--

For years, RxJS has been the backbone of Angular applications. Then Angular introduced Signals, and suddenly one of the biggest architectural decisions every Angular team makes became far less obvious.
Should you abandon RxJS?
Should every new Angular 20 project use Signals?
Can Signals completely replace Observables?
After building and reviewing enterprise Angular applications across SaaS platforms, banking systems, healthcare dashboards, and large web portals, I’ve found that the answer isn’t “either/or.” It’s knowing exactly where each tool belongs.
That distinction can dramatically improve application performance, simplify debugging, and make your codebase significantly easier to maintain.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Angular Signals vs RxJSLet’s break down how experienced Angular teams are approaching this shift.
Why Angular Introduced Signals
Angular wasn’t trying to replace RxJS. It was solving a different problem.
For years, Angular relied heavily on Zone.js to detect UI changes. While effective, this approach often caused unnecessary component updates and made performance optimization more complicated than it needed to be.
Signals introduce fine-grained reactivity. Instead of checking entire component trees, Angular knows exactly which piece of state changed and updates only the affected UI.
That makes change detection more predictable, more efficient, and much easier to reason about.
Think of Signals as Angular’s answer to local reactive state, not asynchronous programming.
Understanding RxJS
RxJS is a reactive programming library built around asynchronous data streams. It excels at situations where data changes over time.
Typical examples include:

• HTTP requests

• WebSockets

• Search autocomplete

• User events

• File uploads

• Timers

• Real-time notifications
Observables aren’t simply variables that change. They’re streams of events.
That distinction matters.
this.products$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
debounceTime(300),
distinctUntilChanged(),
switchMap(term => this.productService.search(term))
);This is exactly the kind of workflow RxJS was designed for. Trying to recreate this with Signals quickly becomes awkward.
Understanding Signals
Signals solve a much narrower problem. They’re reactive values.
const count = signal(0);count.set(1);
count.update(value => value + 1);
Reading a Signal feels almost like reading a normal variable.
count()Computed values are equally elegant.
const price = signal(100);
const tax = signal(18);const total = computed(() => price() + tax());
Whenever either value changes, Angular recalculates only what depends on it.
No subscriptions. No pipes. No manual cleanup.
Signals and RxJS Solve Different Problems
One mistake I see teams make most often is assuming Signals are “the new RxJS.”
They aren’t.
A better mental model is this:
Signals manage application state.
RxJS manages asynchronous workflows.
Once your team understands that distinction, architectural decisions become much easier.
A Simple Comparison
Instead of asking which one is better, ask which problem you’re solving.
Signals are excellent for:

• Component state

• UI state

• Forms

• Derived values

• Local state management

• Angular change detection optimization
RxJS is excellent for:

• API calls

• Event streams

• WebSockets

• Complex async workflows

• Retry logic

• Stream composition

• Real-time applications
Comparing the Same Feature
Using RxJS
private counter = new BehaviorSubject(0);counter$ = this.counter.asObservable();
increment() {
this.counter.next(this.counter.value + 1);
}
Works perfectly. But there’s ceremony.
Using Signals
counter = signal(0);increment() {
this.counter.update(value => value + 1);
}
That’s it. Much simpler. Less code. Easier to debug.
Computed State Is Where Signals Shine
Imagine an e-commerce checkout.
subtotal = signal(500);shipping = signal(50);
discount = signal(100);
total = computed(() =>
subtotal() + shipping() - discount()
);
No subscriptions. No Subjects. No combineLatest.
Angular automatically recalculates only when needed.
This alone removes a surprising amount of complexity from large applications.
When RxJS Is Still the Better Choice
Consider a search box.
results$ = this.search.valueChanges.pipe(
debounceTime(300),
switchMap(query =>
this.api.search(query)
)
);RxJS handles:

• Debouncing

• Cancellation

• Switching requests

• Error handling

• Retry strategies
Signals aren’t trying to replace any of these capabilities.
They Work Extremely Well Together
One of Angular’s smartest decisions was making Signals interoperable with Observables.
products = toSignal(
this.productService.getProducts(),
{ initialValue: [] }
);Or the other direction.
const observable = toObservable(this.counter);This allows incremental migration rather than forcing complete rewrites. That matters in enterprise applications with hundreds of developers.
Performance in Large Applications
Performance discussions often become oversimplified.
Signals aren’t magically faster. They’re more precise.
Instead of triggering broad change detection cycles, Angular updates only components that depend on changed Signals.
In a SaaS analytics dashboard with dozens of widgets, that precision reduces unnecessary rendering.
However, if your bottleneck is network latency or expensive API calls, Signals won’t solve that. RxJS operators like switchMap, shareReplay, and debounceTime still have far greater impact.
Enterprise Architecture Example
Imagine a banking portal.
API Layer
│
▼
RxJS Observables
│
▼
Repository / Services
│
▼
Signals Store
│
▼
Angular ComponentsThis separation works remarkably well.
Services remain asynchronous. Components remain simple. State stays predictable.
Real Production Scenarios
CRM Platform
Customer profile editing. Use Signals. Most state is local. Minimal async complexity.
Healthcare Dashboard
Real-time patient monitoring. RxJS is essential. Continuous event streams are exactly what Observables were built for.
SaaS Analytics Dashboard
Use both. API polling through RxJS. Dashboard state through Signals. This is the architecture I’d recommend today.
Inventory Management System
Signals for UI. RxJS for synchronization with warehouses and inventory updates.
HR Management Software
Forms, employee profiles, and filters all benefit from Signals. Background synchronization remains an RxJS responsibility.
If I Were Starting a New Angular SaaS Today
I wouldn’t eliminate RxJS. I’d reduce how much of it lives inside components.
My architecture would look like this:

• Services expose Observables.

• API logic stays inside RxJS.

• Components consume Signals.

• Computed state uses computed().

• Side effects use effect() sparingly.
This keeps components remarkably clean.
Many enterprise SaaS platforms built by a saas product development company are already moving toward this hybrid approach because it improves maintainability without sacrificing flexibility.
Migration Strategy
Don’t rewrite everything.
Instead:

• Keep existing RxJS services.

• Introduce Signals for new components.

• Replace BehaviorSubject only when it represents local UI state.

• Convert Observables into Signals at component boundaries.

• Measure performance before optimizing.
Incremental migration almost always succeeds. Big-bang rewrites rarely do.
Common Mistakes
Replacing every Observable
Not every stream should become a Signal. Asynchronous workflows still belong in RxJS.
Using effect() everywhere
Effects should remain rare. Most derived state belongs in computed().
Treating Signals like global state
Signals are excellent for component state. Global application state still benefits from structured state management patterns.
Ignoring architecture
The best Angular applications aren’t built around a single technology. They’re built around clear boundaries.
Recommended Project Structure
src/ services/
api.service.ts
stores/
user.store.ts
product.store.ts
components/
pages/
models/
shared/
Keep asynchronous logic inside services. Keep reactive state inside stores. Keep components focused on presentation.
This structure scales well for large-scale Angular apps, whether you’re building internal admin tools or enterprise software with hundreds of screens.
Organizations working with an experienced web portal development company often adopt a similar layered architecture because it separates concerns cleanly and simplifies long-term maintenance.
Production Tips

• Prefer Signals for component state.

• Keep HTTP logic inside services.

• Avoid nested subscriptions.

• Convert Observables into Signals only at the UI layer.

• Use computed() instead of repeated calculations.

• Keep effects minimal.

• Profile before optimizing.

• Design architecture first, choose APIs second.
Decision Framework
Ask these questions.
Is the data asynchronous? Use RxJS.
Is it local component state? Use Signals.
Does it involve event streams? Use RxJS.
Is it derived UI state? Use computed Signals.
Do you need both? Combine them. Most enterprise applications do.
Recommended Learning Path
If you’re new to modern Angular architecture:

• Master TypeScript fundamentals.

• Understand Angular components and Standalone Components.

• Learn Dependency Injection deeply.

• Build confidence with RxJS operators.

• Learn Signals, computed(), and effect().

• Explore Signal-based state management.

• Study Angular optimization and performance profiling.

• Build a production-scale application combining both approaches.
Developers evaluating an angular development company or comparing Angular with a react js development company often focus on frameworks first. In practice, architecture, maintainability, and engineering discipline have a much bigger impact on long-term success than the framework itself.
Similarly, cloud-native Angular applications delivered by a cloud application development company and supported through modern cloud management services typically rely on both Signals and RxJS because each solves a different class of problems.
Frequently Asked Questions
Can Angular Signals replace RxJS?
No. Signals manage reactive state, while RxJS handles asynchronous streams and complex event processing.
Should new Angular 20 projects use Signals?
Yes, especially for component state, forms, and derived values.
Is RxJS still worth learning?
Absolutely. It’s still fundamental for HTTP requests, WebSockets, routing events, and asynchronous workflows.
Are Signals faster than RxJS?
Not directly. Signals enable more efficient UI updates through fine-grained change detection, while RxJS focuses on data flow rather than rendering performance.
Should I replace BehaviorSubject with Signals?
Only if the BehaviorSubject is being used as local UI state. Keep BehaviorSubject where reactive streams are still required.
Can Signals and Observables work together?
Yes. Angular provides toSignal() and toObservable() for seamless interoperability.
Are Signals enough for enterprise applications?
Usually not by themselves. Most enterprise systems combine Signals for state with RxJS for asynchronous communication.
Do Signals eliminate Zone.js?
Signals reduce the need for broad change detection, but Angular’s relationship with Zone.js depends on your application’s configuration and migration strategy.
What is the biggest migration mistake?
Replacing every Observable without understanding why it exists. Migrate based on responsibilities, not trends.
Which approach scales better?
The hybrid approach. RxJS for asynchronous workflows and Signals for reactive UI state consistently produces the cleanest architecture in large production systems.
Final Thoughts
The Signals versus RxJS debate misses the bigger picture.
Experienced Angular teams don’t choose one over the other. They assign each technology the responsibility it handles best.
Signals make components cleaner. RxJS keeps asynchronous workflows powerful.
Together, they create applications that are easier to debug, simpler
